text: Short (<7 days) and long (≥7 days) between-match recovery periods show no significant difference in match-injury risk (7.5% vs 6.8%).

High chronic workloads (18.9-22.0 km weekly) are associated with a reduced risk of match injury compared to lower chronic workloads (<18.9 km) during short recovery periods, with relative risk of 0.27-0.32.
An acute:chronic workload ratio ≥1.6 during short between-match recovery increases match injury risk by 3.4-5.8 times compared to lower ratios.
Acute:chronic workload ratios between 1.2 and 1.6 are associated with greater injury risk than ratios between 1.0 and 1.2 during short recovery periods (RR=2.88).
High chronic workloads may have a protective effect against injury, even when recovery time between matches is short, contradicting the assumption that high workloads always increase injury risk.
Acute:chronic workload ratios around 1.5 represent an elevated injury risk zone, and workloads can be strategically manipulated to decrease injury risk associated with shorter recovery periods.
